1: Then he said to Moses, 'Come up to YHWH (aleh el-YHWH), you and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel': The seventy elders (shiv'im) represent the full council of Israel (cf. Numbers 11:16-17; the seventy nations of Gen 10); Nadab and Abihu are Aaron's eldest sons who will later die for offering unauthorized fire (Lev 10). Their presence here at the covenant ratification makes their subsequent death more tragic — they witnessed the covenant but violated its worship standards. The number seventy = the fullness of the council.
2: Moses alone shall come near to YHWH (ve'nigash Moshe levaddo el-YHWH): Nagash = to draw near (the approach verb for both priests and worshippers); levaddo = alone, by himself. The hierarchy of access is explicit: Moses alone crosses the divine threshold; the elders and Aaron worship from a distance (min-rachok = from afar). The gradation anticipates the tabernacle's concentric zones of holiness.
3: Moses came and told the people all the words of YHWH and all the rules (kol-divrei YHWH ve'et kol-hamishpatim): Divrei YHWH = the words of YHWH (the Decalogue); hamishpatim = the rules/case laws (the Covenant Code of Exodus 21-23). And all the people answered with one voice (kol-echad): 'All the words that YHWH has spoken we will do (na'aseh).' The corporate consent — kol-echad (one voice) — is the ideal of covenant community. Na'aseh is the first of the famous na'aseh v'nishma of Exodus 24:7.
4: And Moses wrote (vayyichtov Moshe) all the words of YHWH: The first explicit statement that Moses wrote the Torah (the words of YHWH); the covenant-writing that forms the basis of the sefer ha-brit (book of the covenant). Writing creates permanence and accountability; the spoken covenant becomes a legal document. He rose early in the morning (vayyashkem baboqer) and built an altar: Rising early (hashkem) is the verb of diligent, devoted action throughout the Pentateuch (Abraham in Genesis 22:3 — the same urgency).
5: And he sent young men (na'are) of the people of Israel, who offered burnt offerings (olot) and sacrificed peace offerings (sh'lamim) of oxen: Na'ar = young man/attendant (not yet the Levitical priesthood — Aaron and his sons have not yet been ordained; these are lay offerers acting on Moses's behalf). Olah = the wholly-consumed burnt offering (from alah, to go up — the offering ascends entirely to YHWH); sh'lamim = peace/fellowship/completeness offerings (from shalom — the offering that restores wholeness and allows communion eating).
6: And Moses took half of the blood (chatzi hadam) and put it in basins, and half of the blood he threw (zaraq) against the altar: The bifurcation of the covenant blood — half on the altar (YHWH's side) and half to be sprinkled on the people (Exodus 24:8) — is the structural act of blood-covenant: both parties are marked with the same blood. Zaraq = to throw/sprinkle (the Levitical term for sacrificial blood-manipulation at the altar). The altar represents YHWH; the people receive the corresponding sprinkle.
7: Then he took the Book of the Covenant (sefer ha-brit) and read it in the hearing of the people: Sefer ha-brit = Book of the Covenant (the first named Torah document — the written record of Exodus 20-23 that becomes the treaty text of the Sinai covenant). And they said, 'All that YHWH has spoken we will do, and we will be obedient (na'aseh v'nishma).' Na'aseh v'nishma = 'we will do and we will hear/obey' — the famous double assent. The order (doing before hearing) has fascinated rabbinic and Christian theology: the rabbis say Israel accepted the covenant before fully understanding it (trust before comprehension); Paul reads it as grace-response before law-understanding.
8: And Moses took the blood and threw it on the people (vayizroq al-ha'am) and said, 'Behold the blood of the covenant (hineh dam ha-brit) that YHWH has made with you in accordance with all these words': Dam ha-brit = blood of the covenant — the defining phrase that Jesus quotes verbatim at the Last Supper (Matthew 26:28: 'this is my blood of the covenant'; cf. Mark 14:24; 1 Corinthians 11:25). The Sinai blood-covenant is the type; Jesus's blood-covenant is the antitype. Moses's blood thrown on the people physically marks them as covenant partners; Jesus's blood covers those who receive it in faith.
9: Then Moses and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel went up: The covenant ratification is followed immediately by covenant communion — the elders ascend to see God. The progression: proclamation (Exodus 24:3) → writing (Exodus 24:4) → sacrifice (Exodus 24:5-6) → consent (Exodus 24:7) → blood-marking (Exodus 24:8) → vision (Exodus 24:9-11) is the complete covenant ratification sequence.
10: And they saw the God of Israel (vayir'u et Elohei Yisrael): The elders see God — the most remarkable statement in the pericope. There was under his feet as it were a pavement of sapphire stone (kivnat ha-sapir), like the very heaven for clearness: Livnat = pavement/brick (from levanah = white/brick; also the word for bricks Israel made in Egypt — the redeemed slaves now see the divine pavement that transcends all human construction). Sapir = sapphire (or lapis lazuli); the heavenly blue pavement recalls Ezekiel 1:26 and Exodus 10:1 (the sapphire firmament under the divine throne). The clarity (kaazem hashamayim latohar = like the very sky for purity) emphasizes the absolute transparency of the divine realm.
11: And he did not lay his hand (lo shalach yado) against the chief men of the people of Israel: The diplomatic understatement — YHWH had the power to destroy them for the audacity of seeing him, but restrained it (cf. Exodus 33:20 — 'no man can see me and live'). The elders' survival is itself a revelation of grace. They beheld God, and ate and drank: The covenant meal eaten in YHWH's presence — the elders eat and drink before God in the same way that a suzerain's covenant vassals shared a table with their overlord. Covenant eating = covenant belonging.
12: YHWH said to Moses, 'Come up to me on the mountain and wait there, that I may give you the tablets of stone (luchot ha'even) with the law and the commandment (ha-Torah ve'ha-mitzvah):' Luchot = tablets/boards; even = stone; Torah = instruction/teaching (the comprehensive term); mitzvah = commandment (the specific directive). The stone tablets inscribed by YHWH (Exodus 31:18 — 'written with the finger of God') are the permanent documentary form of the covenant. That you may teach them (l'horotam): The Hiphil of yarah (to teach/instruct) — the same root as Torah; YHWH gives Moses the Torah to teach Israel.
13: So Moses rose with his assistant Joshua (Yehoshua): This is Joshua's first appearance in Exodus as Moses's mesharet (minister/attendant — from sharath, to serve/minister; the same term for angels ministering before YHWH). Joshua will not go to the summit but accompanies Moses partway; his presence here anticipates his role as Moses's successor and the leader of the conquest.
14: He said to the elders, 'Wait here for us until we return to you. And behold, Aaron and Hur are with you. Whoever has a dispute (mi ba'al devarim), let him go to them.' Ba'al devarim = owner of words/matters (one who has a legal case or dispute); devarim = words/matters/things. Aaron and Hur are left as judicial deputies. Hur (mentioned only here and Exodus 17:10-12 at Rephidim) is traditionally identified as the husband of Miriam or Caleb's son; he disappears from the narrative after this, which has led to speculation about his fate at the golden calf incident.
15: Then Moses went up on the mountain, and the cloud (he'anan) covered the mountain: The cloud (anan) that led Israel through the wilderness now settles on Sinai as the medium of divine presence. The cloud that was a pillar of protection in transit becomes a static covering in theophany.
16: The glory of YHWH (kevod YHWH) dwelt (vayishkon) on Mount Sinai: Kavod = glory/weight; shakan = to dwell/tabernacle (the root of mishkan = tabernacle, and Shekinah = divine dwelling presence). The kavod shakan formula is the technical term for the divine presence settling in a place — the same formula will be used for the tabernacle's dedication (Exodus 40:34-35) and the Temple's dedication (1 Kings 8:11). And the cloud covered it six days: Six days of cloud-covered silence before the seventh — the Sabbath structure of Sinai theophany mirrors the creation week.
17: Now the appearance of the glory of YHWH was like a devouring fire (ke'ur esh ocheleth) on the top of the mountain: Ocheleth = consuming/devouring (from achal = to eat); the glory appears to the Israelites below as a consuming fire — the same image as Deuteronomy 4:24 ('for YHWH your God is a devouring fire') and Hebrews 12:29 ('our God is a consuming fire'). The fire of holiness consumes what is unholy — in the context of covenant it is simultaneously inviting and terrifying.
18: Moses entered the cloud (vayyavo Moshe betoch he'anan) and went up on the mountain: The climax of the approach: Moses enters the cloud — the divine presence itself. He is not destroyed because he goes at YHWH's invitation; the covenant mediator crosses the threshold of the divine holiness under divine commission. And Moses was on the mountain forty days and forty nights (arba'im yom v'arba'im lailah): The first of three forty-day periods at Sinai (here; 34:28 after the golden calf; cf. Deuteronomy 9:18,25). The forty-day pattern becomes the biblical number for a complete cycle of divine preparation: Moses, Elijah at Horeb (1 Kings 19:8), Jesus in the wilderness (Matthew 4:2). Forty = the complete period of testing, transformation, and formation.
